comments.text: Wikipedia says "Computational searches have shown that the origin is separated from infinity by a moat of width 6" and links to this reference: Tsuchimura, Nobuyuki (2005), "Computational results for Gaussian moat problem", IEICE Transactions on Fundamentals of Electronics, Communications and Computer Sciences, 88 (5): 1267–1273, Bibcode:2005IEITF..88.1267T, doi:10.1093/ietfec/e88-a.5.1267. I really enjoyed the article. In the end, the author includes some simple but nice plots. Their computational results are very consistent with a random model of Gaussian primes, for which the answer is "no, such a bounded infinite sequence does not exist".

There is an $M$ and a sequence of Gaussian primes $\{\gamma_j\}_{1^\infty}$, such that $|\gamma_1| < M$, $|\gamma_j - \gamma_{j-1}| < M$ and $\lim |\gamma_j| = \infty$. J. H. Jordan and J. R. Rabung has showed that if the conjecture is true, then $M \geq 4$, in A Conjecture of Paul Erdős Concerning Gaussian Primes(1970). Thanks To Vjeko for pointing the mistakes out!

As far as I can see, Jordan and Rabung did not establish the conjecture (I mean the Gaussian moat problem), but rather ''report that if the conjecture is true then $M\geq4$''. They also performed a computer search that led them to believe that the conjecture *might* be true. Last time I checked the literature, it seemed that the claim was still open.

This fact was stated in a paper of Tuza as "An analogous version of Gaussian moat problem by Erdos was solved by Jordan and Rabung". So, I just had a cursory look on the paper itself. Thanks for clarifying. P.S. This conjecture is new and therefore needs to be included in the site.

I might not have been reading your post carefully either, because now I see that you mention some alternative conjecture, and that it does not make sense. The condition $|\gamma_j|<M$ is in a contradiction with $\lim|\gamma_j|=\infty$. So this was just a typo somewhere! What Jordan and Ranburg actually write is $|\gamma_1|<M$, in which case this is the original "Gaussian moat problem". There was no alternative version of the conjecture at all.
